rows.answer:
Twenty provides both REST and GraphQL APIs, which are automatically generated based on your workspace schema. You can authenticate using API keys or OAuth 2.0.

API keys are created via Settings > API & Webhooks. Use them in the Authorization header:
```http
Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY
```

For OAuth, Twenty supports authorization code flow (with PKCE) for user-facing apps and client credentials for server-to-server access. Clients are registered dynamically via `POST /oauth/register`.

Twenty also includes an official MCP server. Discovery for APIs and MCP is available via `/.well-known/` endpoints on your workspace host.

rows.answer:
DealCloud offers REST APIs and a specialized Model Context Protocol (MCP) server for AI integration [1][2][3].

To access the REST API, administrators must enable API access for a user group within the Admin section, after which users can generate an API key via their profile [4]. Authentication uses the OAuth2 Client Credentials flow, where you exchange a client_id and apiKey at the /api/rest/v1/oauth/token endpoint for a bearer token [1].

```
POST {baseUrl}/api/rest/v1/oauth/token
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
 
scope=data user_management
&grant_type=client_credentials
&client_id=106
&client_secret=1356GYNU
```

The DealCloud MCP server, available as of July 2026, supports GraphQL for data retrieval [1][5]. It uses an OAuth sign-in flow where users authorize AI assistants directly, maintaining their existing permission context [6][7]. Tools include search and entry lookup [5]. Webhooks are not documented in the provided search results.

Authentication for both the REST API and the MCP server relies solely on a Freshdesk API key. Access to the MCP server is restricted to the Enterprise plan via an Early Access Program requiring approval from a technical account manager. Standard API access is available to all users, subject to plan-based rate limits. No official GraphQL support exists.

rows.answer:
Gorgias provides a REST API for integrations. Private apps use API key authentication, while public apps must use OAuth2 [1][2][3].

For private apps, generate an API key via Settings in your Gorgias account [2]. Authenticate using HTTP Basic Auth with your email as the username and your API key as the password, provided as a base64 encoded string in the Authorization header [2].

```bash
curl --request GET \
  --url https://your-customer-account.gorgias.com/api/account \
  --header 'Authorization: Basic base64encode(USERNAME:API_KEY)'
```

For public apps, register on the Gorgias Developer Portal, create a draft app, and contact techpartners@gorgias.com to convert your trial account into a Sandbox account [4][5]. Public apps use the OAuth2 authorization code flow to obtain a Bearer access token [6][7].

```bash
curl --request GET \
  --url https://your-customer-account.gorgias.com/api/account \
  --header 'Authorization: Bearer YOUR-ACCESS-TOKEN'
```

Gorgias does not have an official MCP server. Provider-owned sources are used, and GraphQL support is unknown.

Telegram provides three distinct APIs: the Bot API, the Telegram (MTProto) API, and the Gateway API [1]. None of these APIs use OAuth; they rely on unique authentication tokens or hashes [2][3][4][5]. 

The Bot API uses a unique token obtained via @BotFather [3][5]. The Gateway API requires a token from your Telegram Gateway account settings, passed via an Authorization: Bearer header or an access_token parameter [2][4]. The MTProto API requires an api_id and api_hash obtained from the my.telegram.org developer portal [6].

There is no GraphQL support; these are REST-based or MTProto-based [2][3]. Webhooks are supported for both the Bot API and the Gateway API [2][3]. No official MCP server is provided. Authentication is handled via Basic Auth (for personal use) using an API ID and token generated in the dashboard [4][3], or OAuth (for multi-customer/partner apps) [2][5]. To use OAuth, you must register as a technology partner by emailing marketplace@aircall.io to request credentials [6].

rows.answer:
Vonage provides communication APIs that are REST-based with JSON payloads. You can sign up for a free account at the Vonage Developer Portal, which includes initial free credit. Administrative approval or partner access is not generally required for standard developer sign-up.

Authentication depends on the API:
- Basic Authentication uses your API Key and Secret, sent as a Base64-encoded string in the Authorization header: `Authorization: Basic base64(API_KEY:API_SECRET)`.
- JSON Web Token (JWT) authentication is used for most products (Voice, Messages, Verify V2) via the `Authorization: Bearer <JWT>` header.

Vonage applications act as containers for security settings and are required for JWTs and webhooks. You can manage these via the Developer Dashboard, the Vonage CLI, or the Application API. Vonage also provides an MCP server to expose documentation and API capabilities to AI agents.

rows.answer:
The Google Ads API is a gRPC and REST-based service that facilitates programmatic management of Google Ads accounts [1]. It does not support API keys or basic tokens for authentication; instead, it requires OAuth 2.0 and a developer token [2][3].

Requests must include the following headers: 
- Authorization: Bearer ACCESS_TOKEN 
- developer-token: DEVELOPER_TOKEN 
- login-customer-id: MANAGER_CUSTOMER_ID (required when accessing client accounts via a manager) [2][1]

The API is free to access for testing, but production usage requires an application process for Basic or Standard access levels [4][5][6]. Partner access involves additional headers [1]. No official MCP server exists in the provided evidence.

rows.answer:
Meta Marketing API authentication uses OAuth 2.0 with access tokens, not API keys or Basic tokens. All API calls require an access token passed as a parameter. You generate tokens using either a User access token flow or a System User access token flow for server-to-server interactions.

The primary endpoint for exchanging an authorization code for a token is:

```
GET https://graph.facebook.com/v25.0/oauth/access_token?
  client_id=<APP_ID>
  &redirect_uri=<URL>
  &client_secret=<APP_SECRET>
  &code=<CODE>
```

Developer signup is free via the Meta for Developers portal. Access tiers (Limited vs. Full) are determined by App Review and API usage metrics. Partner access is managed through permissions and business-to-business authorizations. No official MCP server is documented. Provider-owned sources are supported via system users.

rows.query: Salesforce Commerce Cloud official developer documentation; API authentication OAuth API key Basic token; developer signup free trial pricing admin approval partner access; REST GraphQL webhooks official MCP server; Use provider-owned sources. State unknown when evidence is absent.
rows.answer:
Salesforce Commerce API (SCAPI) provides RESTful APIs for storefronts and merchant tools [1]. Authentication depends on the API type: Admin APIs use OAuth 2.0 via Account Manager [2][3], while Shopper APIs use the Shopper Login and API Access Service (SLAS), which follows OAuth 2.1 [4][5].

To authenticate Admin APIs, obtain a JWT access token from Account Manager using `client_credentials` grant type [2].

```bash
curl "https://account.demandware.com/dwsso/oauth2/access_token" \
  --request 'POST' \
  --user "$CLIENT_ID:$CLIENT_SECRET" \
  --header 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
  --data "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  --data-urlencode "scope=SALESFORCE_COMMERCE_API:${REALM_ID}_${INSTANCE_ID} $SCOPES"
```

SCAPI includes a Developer Preview MCP server for IDE integration [1][6]. Access details regarding free trials, admin approval, and partner access are unknown based on the provided search results. SCAPI is available to B2C Commerce customers at no extra cost [1].

Binance provides official REST and WebSocket APIs for developers [1][2][3]. Authentication is handled via HMAC, RSA, or Ed25519 API keys [1][3], or OAuth 2.0, which is currently reserved for close ecosystem partners and requires business team contact [4][5]. Developer signup is available through the Binance Developer Center [4][5], though there is no information regarding free trial pricing or general public admin approval beyond standard account management.

Binance does not offer an official Model Context Protocol (MCP) server.

For API access, create keys via the Binance API Management page [1][3]. Most secure endpoints require a SIGNED request containing a timestamp and signature [1][3].

Example REST signature usage:
```text
curl -H "X-MBX-APIKEY: YOUR_API_KEY" 'https://api.binance.com/api/v3/order?symbol=BTCUSDT&side=BUY&type=LIMIT&timeInForce=GTC&quantity=1&price=1&timestamp=1600000000000&signature=YOUR_SIGNATURE'
```

Public REST endpoints use `https://api.binance.com` [1], and WebSocket streams use `wss://ws-api.binance.com:443/ws-api/v3` [3].
